column1: PlanNav – Master Question Table (Machine-Readable Flat Export)

column4: question_text
column6: clause_meaning
column7: routing_logic
column11: guidance_text

column4: Is your property a listed building, officially recognised for its special architectural or historic interest?
column5: Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990, ss.7 & 9
column6: Any works affecting a listed building require Listed Building Consent; PD rights do not apply.
column7: If Yes → your project will need special consent and cannot rely on standard permitted development rights. If No → you can continue with normal planning rules.
column11: Permitted Development rights do not apply to listed buildings. Any works will require Listed Building Consent and possibly planning permission, regardless of scale or type.

column4: Is your property located in a conservation area, which is a special area recognised for its character or history?
column5: Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990, ss.69 & 72
column6: Works affecting conservation areas may require planning permission; PD rights are restricted.
column7: If Yes → some works may need full planning permission, and additional checks may apply. If No → standard planning rules apply.
column11: Permitted Development rights are more limited in conservation areas. Some works may still be allowed, but additional restrictions apply. A Lawful Development Certificate can confirm whether PD applies.

column4: Has your property been specifically restricted by your local council so that some standard planning rights no longer apply? (Article 4)
column6: Local authorities can withdraw specific PD rights in defined areas; works that would normally be PD may require permission.
column7: If Yes → some types of work that are normally allowed will require planning permission. If No → you can continue with normal permitted development rules.
column11: An Article 4 Direction removes specific Permitted Development rights. Even minor works may need planning permission. Check the scope of the direction or confirm via a Lawful Development Certificate (LDC).

column4: Is your property located within a protected area, such as a National Park, Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty, or World Heritage Site?
column6: PD rights are restricted on designated land (National Parks, AONB, WHS); extra conditions apply.
column7: If Yes → extra limits apply and some work may require full planning permission. If No → normal planning rules apply.
column11: Permitted Development rights are more restricted on designated land. Some development may still be possible, but tighter limits apply and planning permission is more likely. An LDC can help confirm.

column4: Are you planning to build a completely new house on this site?
column5: GPDO 2015 does not permit new dwellings
column6: New dwellings are not PD; full planning permission required.
column7: If Yes → full planning permission likely required. If No → next question
column11: Permitted Development rights do not apply to new dwellings. Full planning permission will be required.

column4: Are you planning to replace an existing house with a new one?
column5: GPDO 2015 does not permit replacement dwellings with footprint changes
column6: PD does not allow replacement if footprint changes; planning permission required.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required if footprint/layout changes. If No → next question
column11: Permitted Development rights do not apply if footprint or layout changes. Planning permission required.

column4: Will you be adding extensions or additional buildings to the existing property?
column5: GPDO 2015, Schedule 2, Part 1, Classes A, AA
column6: Extensions and alterations may be PD subject to size limits, conditions, and exceptions.
column7: If Yes → Permitted Development may apply, some checks needed. If No → next question

column4: Will you be converting the property or changing its use (e.g., residential to commercial or flats)?
column6: Certain change-of-use classes may be PD, subject to conditions and limits.
column7: If Yes → planning permission may be needed, check PD rules. If No → next question

column4: Will your project involve demolishing an existing building or carrying out site works like fences, walls, gates, or driveways?
column6: Minor works may be PD; demolition often requires planning permission and may be restricted in conservation areas.
column7: If Yes → planning permission may be required; check building control. If No → next question

column4: Are you planning a loft extension?
column6: Enlargement, improvement, or alteration of dwellinghouse; max 40m³ in conservation areas, 50m³ elsewhere.
column7: If Yes → Permitted Development may apply, building control needed. If No → next question

column4: Are you planning a single-storey extension?
column6: Single-storey extension; max 4m for detached, 3m for others; max 50% of curtilage coverage; PD conditions apply.
column7: If Yes → Permitted Development may apply, building control needed. If No → next question

column4: Are you planning a multi-storey extension?
column5: GPDO 2015, Schedule 2, Part 1, Class A / AA
column6: Multi-storey extensions often require permission unless within strict size/height limits; PD restrictions apply.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required, building control needed. If No → next question
column11: Multi-storey extensions usually cannot be done under permitted development. You will need full planning permission, and Building Control approval is required to ensure the structure is safe. Professional advice is strongly recommended before starting.

column4: Are you planning a conservatory or sunroom?
column6: Conservatories treated as single-storey extensions; size limits and PD conditions apply.
column7: If Yes → PD may apply, building control needed. If No → next question

column4: Are you planning a new garage or converting an existing garage?
column6: Outbuildings incidental to dwelling; max 50% of curtilage; height limits apply; PD conditions.
column7: If Yes → PD may apply; building control required if habitable. If No → next question

column4: Are you planning any external building like a workshop, garden office, or pool building?
column6: Outbuildings incidental to dwelling; size, height, and location limits; PD conditions apply.
column7: If Yes → PD may apply; building control required if serviced/habitable. If No → next question

column4: Are you planning to add a balcony?
column6: Balconies usually not PD; planning permission required; PD only applies for minor roof alterations in limited cases.
column7: If Yes → planning permission and building control required. If No → next question
column11: Adding a balcony is generally not allowed under permitted development. Planning permission is required, and Building Control approval is needed for structural safety. Seek professional advice to confirm requirements and options.

column4: Are you planning to add a porch?
column6: Porch permitted up to 3m high and 3m from original dwelling; PD conditions apply.
column7: If Yes → PD may apply if small; building control usually required. If No → next question

column4: Are you planning an annex or granny flat?
column6: Ancillary buildings; PD conditions include single-storey, max height, curtilage limits, not in front of principal elevation.
column7: If Yes → PD may apply if ancillary; building control required. If No → next question

column4: Are you converting a single dwelling into flats or bedsits?
column6: Change of use from dwelling to multiple dwellings may be permitted in some areas; conditions apply.
column7: If Yes → planning permission and Building Control required. If No → next question
column11: Planning permission is required. You cannot use permitted rights. Building Control approval is also required before starting work. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Are you converting flats into a single dwelling?
column6: Converting multiple dwellings into one may be permitted subject to conditions.
column7: If Yes → may be allowed; Building Control required. If No → next question

column4: Are you converting a single dwelling into a House in Multiple Occupation (HMO)?
column6: Some HMO conversions may be permitted; local licensing/fire safety conditions apply.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; check local housing and safety rules. If No → next question
column11: Planning permission is required. You must check any local rules for housing or safety. Building Control may also be required. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Are you converting a commercial building into residential?
column6: PD allowed from office to residential under Class MA; conditions include size, prior approval, and limits.
column7: If Yes → submit prior approval application; Building Control required. If No → next question
column11: You may be able to convert this property under Permitted Development rights. Submit a prior approval application to your council. You can also request a Lawful Development Certificate. Building Control approval may also be required. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Are you converting space above shops into residential?
column6: Change of use from commercial/retail to residential may be permitted under Class O; prior approval required.
column7: If Yes → submit prior approval application; Building Control required. If No → next question
column11: You may be able to convert the space above a shop under Permitted Development rights. Submit a prior approval application to your council. Building Control approval may also be required. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Are you converting a residential building into commercial use?
column6: Residential to commercial change generally requires planning permission; PD exceptions limited.
column7: If Yes → planning permission and Building Control required. If No → next question
column11: Planning permission is required. You cannot use permitted rights. Building Control approval is also required. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Are you making a general change of use to the property?
column6: Some changes of use may be PD; prior approval and conditions apply.
column7: If Yes → submit prior approval application; Building Control may be required. If No → next question
column11: Some general changes of use may be allowed without full planning permission. Submit a prior approval application to your council. Building Control may also be required. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Will your project involve demolishing a building?
column5: GPDO 2015, Schedule 2, Part 11 / local planning rules
column6: Demolition often requires permission; PD does not generally cover demolition in conservation areas.
column7: If Yes → planning permission often required; building control required. If No → next question
column11: Planning permission is often required, particularly in conservation areas. Building Control approval is also required. Professional advice is recommended to ensure all requirements are met.

column4: Will your project involve other site works like fences, walls, gates, or driveways?
column5: GPDO 2015, Schedule 2, Part 2, Classes A & F
column6: Minor operations (fences, walls, gates) and hard surfaces may be PD; limits on height, location, and materials apply.
column7: If Yes → some works may be permitted; check height/SuDS; building control rarely applies. If No → next question
column11: Some minor site works may be allowed without full planning permission. You should check local rules on height, location, and materials. Building Control rarely applies. Professional advice is recommended if unsure.

column4: Is the loft a Mansard roof (a roof with two slopes on each side, the lower one being steeper)?
column6: Certain roof types may remove PD eligibility.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Mansard roofs are not allowed under permitted development rights. Planning permission is required, and professional advice is recommended to confirm requirements.

column4: Does the loft raise the height of the walls at the edge of the roof (eaves) above the original?
column6: Increasing eaves above original height may remove PD.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Raising the eaves above the original height removes permitted development rights. Planning permission is required, and professional advice is recommended.

column4: Will the total new space created by the loft exceed 40m³ in a conservation area or 50m³ elsewhere?
column6: Volume limits: 40m³ in conservation, 50m³ elsewhere.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → generally allowed, building control required
column11: If the total new loft space exceeds 40m³ in a conservation area or 50m³ elsewhere → planning permission is required. If it is under these limits → generally allowed, but Building Control approval may still be needed.

column4: Is your property in a conservation area or a listed building (protected for historic or architectural interest)?
column5: Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990
column6: Works in conservation areas or listed buildings are restricted.
column7: If Yes → planning permission / LDC required; If No → continue
column11: If the property is in a conservation area or is listed, permitted development rights do not apply. Submit a Lawful Development Certificate or planning application. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Will the extension project more than 4m if detached, or 3m if semi/terraced?
column6: Projection limits differ by property type.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: If the extension projects more than 4m (detached) or 3m (semi/terraced), planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended to confirm limits.

column4: Will the extension exceed 4m in height?
column6: Height limit: 4m for single-storey.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Single-storey extensions over 4m high are not allowed under permitted development rights. Planning permission is required, and professional advice is recommended.

column4: Will the total area of extensions and outbuildings exceed 50% of the garden/curtilage?
column6: Total extension must not cover more than 50% of curtilage.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: If the total area of extensions and outbuildings exceeds 50% of the curtilage, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended if unsure.

column4: Is the extension at the rear of the property and not in front of the main elevation?
column6: Must be to rear; not in front of main elevation.
column7: If No → planning permission required; If Yes → continue
column11: Extensions must be to the rear of the property. Front extensions require planning permission. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Is your property in a conservation area or a listed building (protected for historic or architectural interest)?
column5: Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990
column6: Extensions in conservation areas or on listed buildings are restricted.
column7: If Yes → planning permission / LDC required; If No → continue
column11: Properties in conservation areas or listed buildings cannot rely on permitted development rights. Submit a Lawful Development Certificate or planning application. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Will the conservatory project more than 4m if detached, or 3m if semi/terraced?
column6: Max projection: 4m for detached, 3m for others.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: If the conservatory projects more than the allowed distance, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended to confirm measurements.

column4: Will the conservatory exceed 4m in height?
column6: Height limit: 4m (single-storey).
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Conservatories over 4 metres in height require planning permission. Professional advice is recommended to confirm height measurements.

column4: Will the total area of the conservatory plus other extensions/outbuildings exceed 50% of the garden/curtilage?
column6: Total area of extensions/outbuildings must not exceed 50% of curtilage.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: If extensions and outbuildings cover more than half of the original garden area, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended if boundaries or previous works are unclear.

column4: Is any part of the conservatory within 2m of the side boundary and taller than 2.5m?
column6: Must be at least 2m from side boundaries if height >2.5m.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: If the conservatory is close to a boundary and exceeds the height limit, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended where boundary positions are uncertain.

column4: Is your property in a conservation area or a listed building?
column5: Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990
column6: Conservatories in conservation areas or on listed buildings are restricted.
column7: If Yes → PD does not apply, LDC / planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Properties in conservation areas or listed buildings cannot rely on permitted development rights. Submit a Lawful Development Certificate or planning application.

column4: Will the garage be converted into a habitable space (bedroom, office, etc.) or serviced (plumbing, kitchen, etc.)?
column6: Habitable or serviced outbuildings may require building control.
column7: If Yes → building control required; planning rules depend on other limits. If No → continue
column11: Converting a garage into a habitable or serviced space normally requires Building Control approval to ensure safety and energy standards are met. Planning permission may still not be required if all other limits are met. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Will the garage exceed 4m in height (or 3m if within 2m of boundary)?
column6: Height limit: 4m (3m if within 2m of boundary).
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: If the garage exceeds the allowed height, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended where measurements or boundary positions are unclear.

column4: Will the total area of the garage plus other outbuildings/extensions exceed 50% of the garden/curtilage?
column6: Total outbuildings must not cover more than 50% of curtilage.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: If outbuildings and extensions cover more than half of the original garden area, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended if previous works are uncertain.

column4: Will the garage be used as a separate dwelling or business?
column6: Must remain incidental to dwelling.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Using a garage as a separate home or business is not automatically allowed and planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended to confirm acceptable use.

column4: Is your property in a conservation area or a listed building?
column5: Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990
column6: Works in conservation areas or on listed buildings are restricted.
column7: If Yes → PD does not apply, LDC / planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Properties in conservation areas or listed buildings cannot rely on permitted development rights. Submit a Lawful Development Certificate or planning application.

column4: Will the building be habitable or include services (plumbing, kitchen, heating, electrics)?
column6: Habitable or serviced buildings require building control.
column7: If Yes → building control required; planning rules depend on other limits. If No → continue
column11: If the building will be used as a habitable space or includes services, Building Control approval is normally required. Planning permission may still not be required if all other limits are met. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Will the building exceed 4m in height (or 3m if within 2m of a boundary)?
column6: Max height: 4m (3m if within 2m of boundary).
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Buildings that exceed the height limits require planning permission. Professional advice is recommended where height measurements or boundary distances are unclear.

column4: Will the total area of this building plus other extensions/outbuildings exceed 50% of the garden/curtilage?
column6: Total outbuildings must not cover more than 50% of curtilage.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: If outbuildings and extensions cover more than half of the original garden area, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended if previous development is unclear.

column4: Is the building located at the rear or side of the house and at least 2m from any side boundary if taller than 2.5m?
column6: Must not be in front of the house; maintain distance from boundaries.
column7: If No → planning permission required; If Yes → continue
column11: Buildings located in front of the house or too close to boundaries require planning permission. Professional advice is recommended where site layout or boundaries are uncertain.

column4: Is your property in a conservation area or a listed building?
column5: Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990
column6: Outbuildings in conservation areas or on listed buildings are restricted.
column7: If Yes → PD does not apply, LDC / planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Properties in conservation areas or listed buildings cannot rely on permitted development rights. Submit a Lawful Development Certificate or planning application.

column4: Will the porch project more than 3m from the original wall?
column6: Max projection: 3m.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: If the porch projects more than 3 metres from the original wall, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended if measurements are unclear.

column4: Will the porch exceed 3m in height?
column6: Max height: 3m.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Porches taller than 3 metres require planning permission. Professional advice is recommended to confirm height.

column4: Will the porch cover more than 50% of the frontage of the house?
column6: Must not cover more than 50% of the frontage.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: If the porch would cover more than half of the frontage of the house, planning permission is required. Professional advice is recommended if frontage boundaries are unclear.

column4: Is your property in a conservation area or a listed building?
column5: Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990
column6: Works in conservation areas or on listed buildings are restricted.
column7: If Yes → PD does not apply, LDC / planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Properties in conservation areas or listed buildings cannot rely on permitted development rights. Submit a Lawful Development Certificate or planning application.

column4: Will the building be used as a separate dwelling or business?
column6: Must be ancillary to main dwelling.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: The annex must not be used as a separate dwelling or business. Professional advice is recommended if intended use is unclear.

column4: Will the annex be more than one storey?
column6: PD applies only for single-storey.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Annexes taller than one storey require planning permission. Professional advice is recommended if plans are ambiguous.

column4: Will the annex exceed 4 metres in height (or 3 metres if within 2 metres of a boundary)?
column6: Maximum height: 4m, 3m if within 2m of boundary.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Buildings exceeding these heights require planning permission. Professional advice is recommended for exact measurement.

column4: Will the total area of the annex plus other outbuildings/extensions exceed 50% of the garden/curtilage?
column6: Must not cover more than 50% of the garden/curtilage.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Annex plus other outbuildings must not exceed half of the garden. Professional advice recommended if boundaries are unclear.

column4: Is the annex located at the front of the main dwelling?
column6: Must be at rear or side of main dwelling.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Annexes in front of the main dwelling are not PD. Professional advice is recommended if site layout is unclear.

column4: Is your property in a conservation area or a listed building?
column5: Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990
column6: Ancillary buildings in conservation areas or on listed buildings are restricted.
column7: If Yes → PD does not apply, LDC / planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Properties in conservation areas or listed buildings cannot rely on permitted development rights. Submit a Lawful Development Certificate or planning application.

column3: Project Specific: Flat Conversion (Multiple Flats to One Home)
column4: Are you converting more than one flat into a single home?
column6: The use of a building as a single dwellinghouse instead of multiple flats can be permitted.
column7: If Yes → the change of use is permitted development; Building Control approval is required; If No → continue
column11: Converting more than one flat into a single home is allowed as permitted development, provided no other works require planning permission. Building Control approval is always required, and professional advice is recommended to confirm compliance.

column3: Project Specific: Flat Conversion (Multiple Flats to One Home)
column4: Will any extension exceed 4 metres in height, or 3 metres if it is within 2 metres of a boundary?
column6: Height restrictions apply to extensions.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Extensions that exceed these height limits are not allowed under permitted development. Professional advice is recommended if measurements are unclear.

column3: Project Specific: Flat Conversion (Multiple Flats to One Home)
column4: Will extensions and outbuildings cover more than 50 percent of the garden or curtilage?
column6: Extensions must not cover more than half of the garden.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: The total area of extensions and outbuildings must not cover more than half of the garden. Professional advice is recommended if boundaries are unclear.

column3: Project Specific: Flat Conversion (Multiple Flats to One Home)
column4: Will any extension be more than one storey?
column6: Extensions are limited by number of storeys.
column7: If Yes → planning permission required; If No → continue
column11: Extensions of more than one storey are not allowed under permitted development. Professional advice is recommended.

column3: Project Specific: Flat Conversion (Multiple Flats to One Home)
column4: Will the conversion involve external changes such as new windows, doors, extensions, or changes to the roof?
column6: External changes may require planning permission.
column7: If Yes → planning permission may be required; If No → continue
column11: External changes are likely to require planning permission if they alter the appearance of the building, add new openings, extend the building, or affect the front or roof. Professional advice is recommended before making any external changes.

column3: Project Specific: Flat Conversion (Multiple Flats to One Home)
column4: Does the conversion involve structural work?
column6: Structural alterations require Building Control approval.
column7: If Yes → Building Control approval required; If No → continue
column11: Any structural changes must meet Building Control requirements. Professional advice is recommended.

column3: Project Specific: Flat Conversion (Multiple Flats to One Home)
column4: Will the conversion affect fire safety or access, for example where the building has more than one storey?
column6: Fire safety and access standards must be met.
column7: If Yes → Building Control approval or professional advice required; If No → continue
column11: Fire safety and safe access must be addressed as part of the conversion. Building Control approval is required, and professional advice is strongly recommended.

column4: Is your site in a flood risk area?
column6: PD rights may be limited in flood risk areas; Building Control requirements may change.
column7: If Yes → flood risk assessment may be required; affects building control. If No → continue
column11: If your site is in a flood risk area, a Flood Risk Assessment may be required to comply with regulations and ensure safe design. Building Control requirements may change. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Will your project affect highway access, parking, or visibility?
column6: PD rights may be restricted if highway safety impacted; approval required.
column7: If Yes → transport assessment or highways approval may be required. If No → continue
column11: If your project affects access, parking, or visibility, a transport assessment or highways approval may be needed to protect safety. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Is your site potentially contaminated?
column6: PD rights may be restricted on contaminated sites; investigation/remediation may be required.
column7: If Yes → site investigation or remediation may be required. If No → continue
column11: If your site may be contaminated, a site investigation or remediation may be required to make it safe. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Will your project affect protected habitats, ecology, or trees?
column5: Wildlife & Countryside Act 1981 / Tree Preservation Orders
column6: PD rights limited if protected species, habitats, or trees affected; surveys may be required.
column7: If Yes → ecological survey or arboricultural assessment may be required. If No → continue
column11: If your project affects habitats, protected species, or trees, an ecological survey or arboricultural assessment may be required. Professional advice is recommended.

column4: Is your site in an area of archaeological interest?
column6: PD rights restricted if archaeological interest present; assessment may be required.
column7: If Yes → desk-based or on-site archaeological assessment may be required. If No → continue
column11: If your site is in an area of archaeological interest, a desk-based or on-site archaeological assessment may be required. Professional advice is recommended.
